## SECTION 1 — Global Pulse (Geopolitics & World Economy)

The ongoing US-Iran conflict saw a significant escalation today, April 18, 2026, as Iran reversed its decision to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, reimposing restrictions on the critical waterway and reportedly firing upon vessels. This action followed US President Donald Trump's reaffirmation that the American blockade on Iranian ports would remain in effect until a deal is reached with Tehran, including on its nuclear program. This standoff contributes to global economic uncertainty and potential energy market disruption. Separately, a diplomatic dispute between Mexico and Spain, stemming from Mexico's demand for an apology for colonial abuses, officially concluded today in Barcelona. Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum and Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez met, with Sheinbaum declaring there was "no diplomatic crisis". Meanwhile, Multilateral Development Banks (MDBs) are enhancing their collaboration to navigate global uncertainties, including the ongoing Middle East situation, to support stability and development in member economies. The International Monetary Fund (IMF) projects global GDP growth to slow to 3.0-3.1% in 2026, citing high energy prices, trade tensions, and the Middle East conflict as major contributing factors.

## SECTION 2 — Global News (Complete Continental Coverage)

### Americas
On Saturday, April 18, 2026, US President Donald Trump announced reforms to accelerate access to medical research and treatment utilizing psychedelic drugs, signing an executive order for the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to expedite the review of drugs such as ibogaine, while committing $50 million to federal research in this area. Concurrently, a US appeals court ruled on Friday, April 17, 2026, that the Trump administration is cleared to continue constructing a $400 million White House ballroom at the former East Wing site. In Canada, new, stricter asylum regulations were introduced today, April 18, 2026, under Bill C-12, a law passed by the Mark Carney government. This legislation could impact nearly 30,000 applicants by imposing new limits on refugee claims, including a retroactive application to June 2020. Canadians in Nova Scotia are observing the sixth anniversary of the country's deadliest mass shooting, which tragically claimed 22 lives in rural Nova Scotia. Additionally, the Government of Canada announced federal support today, April 18, 2026, for Soccer Nova Scotia to upgrade the BMO Soccer Centre, aiming to provide a professional, year-round training environment for the Halifax Tides FC women's soccer team. Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum met with Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez in Barcelona today, April 18, 2026, formally resolving a diplomatic spat concerning Spain's colonial past. Latin American financial markets are currently experiencing significant volatility, influenced by geopolitical tensions, monetary policy uncertainties, and upcoming electoral pressures.

### Europe
On Saturday, April 18, 2026, British counterterrorism police are investigating an attempted arson attack on a building in London that was formerly used by the Jewish community. This is part of a series of incidents targeting Jewish and Iranian premises in the city, with authorities exploring potential links to Iran. A domestic political discussion in the UK is also focusing on the definition of marmalade as Prime Minister Keir Starmer seeks closer ties with the European Union, which could lead to an alignment of food regulations. In Germany, voters in the Rhine/Ruhr region are set to decide tomorrow, April 19, 2026, on a potential bid to host the Summer Olympics in 2036, 2040, or 2044. Earlier in 2026, Germany saw a notable increase in building permissions for new dwellings, with 41,700 apartments approved in the first two months, representing a rise of 5,800 compared to the previous year. However, a powerful explosion inside a pedestrian tunnel in Volklingen, western Germany, resulted in one fatality and four serious injuries today, with investigators suspecting human action. The European Commission (EC) today approved €4.2 billion in state aid programs for energy-intensive companies in Bulgaria, Germany, and Slovenia to reduce electricity costs and promote decarbonization. Furthermore, European Union governments are developing plans to accelerate their transition to clean energy in response to recent fossil fuel price shocks.

The ongoing conflict in Ukraine saw renewed Russian attacks overnight on April 18, 2026, resulting in one civilian death and dozens wounded, including a strike on port infrastructure in Odesa. Ukraine's air force reported intercepting 190 out of 219 Russian drones. Concurrently, Ukrainian drone strikes targeted significant Russian oil industry facilities in the Samara, Leningrad, and Krasnodar regions. Russia's government has indicated a reduction in new business support programs, citing increasing fiscal pressures. Meanwhile, France has deployed three types of interceptor drones to its Middle Eastern bases in Abu Dhabi to counter Iranian Shahed drones, aiming for cost-effective solutions informed by Ukraine's experience.

### Asia-Pacific
On Saturday, April 18, 2026, Australia and Japan formalized a A$10 billion (US$6.5 billion) deal for Japan to supply warships to Australia, marking Japan's largest military sale since 2014. This agreement will see the first three Mogami-class frigates built in Japan, with eight more to be constructed in Australia. Japan also allocated approximately ¥10.6 trillion for security-related expenditures in its fiscal 2026 budget, moving closer to its goal of 2% of GDP for defense spending by fiscal 2027. A magnitude 5.0 earthquake struck Japan's Nagano and surrounding prefectures today, with no tsunami expected. Meanwhile, China's military conducted joint combat readiness patrols in the East China Sea, a day after a Japanese warship transited the Taiwan Strait, indicating heightened regional tensions. China also concluded its sixth International Consumer Products Expo in Hainan, which showcased over 3,400 brands from more than 60 countries. In Southeast Asia, economies are accelerating renewable energy initiatives in response to the global energy crisis and disruptions in West Asia, leading to a surge in electric vehicle sales.

### Africa
The Democratic Republic of Congo is projected to become sub-Saharan Africa's fifth-largest economy in 2026, surpassing Ethiopia with an estimated GDP of $123 billion, according to the International Monetary Fund (IMF). The IMF's April 2026 Regional Economic Outlook also indicates a weakening fiscal position for Sub-Saharan Africa, with the median fiscal deficit expected to widen to 3.2% of GDP in 2026, partly due to the Iran crisis impacting fuel-importing nations. Pope Leo XIV arrived in Angola on Saturday, April 18, 2026, as part of his four-nation Africa tour, delivering a message of peace and addressing issues of exploitation. Morocco is emerging as a significant defense-tech hub, having inaugurated an Israeli-designed drone production facility in November 2025. Ethiopia, under its April 2026 chairship, led a high-level African Union Peace and Security Council meeting on April 17, 2026, to discuss the implications of artificial intelligence for continental governance, peace, and security. Somalia's ambassador to Ethiopia issued a warning on Friday, April 17, 2026, against foreign interference in its internal affairs, citing potential restrictions on access to the Bab-el-Mandeb Strait following Israel's appointment of an ambassador to Somaliland.

On Saturday, April 18, 2026, the Strait of Hormuz once again became a flashpoint as Iran reversed its decision to reopen the vital waterway, reimposing restrictions and reportedly firing upon commercial vessels. This escalation followed US President Donald Trump's assertion that the American naval blockade on Iranian ports would remain in full effect. The incident has intensified concerns over global energy supplies and prompted calls for diplomatic intervention. Despite this, Pakistani officials indicate ongoing efforts to facilitate US-Iran peace talks, with a second round of negotiations anticipated in Islamabad on Monday, April 20, 2026, though Iranian officials stated no date has been set. The brief opening of the Strait on Friday had led to a significant drop in global oil prices, which are now expected to remain volatile.

In Lebanon, a French soldier serving with UN peacekeepers was killed and three others injured in an attack in the southern region today, April 18, 2026. French President Emmanuel Macron attributed blame to Hezbollah. This incident occurred despite a 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah, which began on Thursday, April 16, 2026. China has proposed a four-point plan aimed at promoting peace and stability in the Middle East, emphasizing de-escalation and the maintenance of international shipping security. ## SECTION 6 — Science & Research Breakthroughs

In physics, scientists have observed singularities in combined light and sound waves moving faster than light, a breakthrough that could reveal hidden processes in various scientific fields. Separately, quantum entanglement between two moving atoms was observed for the first time, opening new avenues for a unified "theory of everything". Researchers also successfully captured virtual particles transitioning from a vacuum into detectable matter, addressing a long-standing mystery regarding the origin of proton mass. A new theory suggests that mysterious "red dot" galaxies may harbor "relic" black holes predating the Big Bang.

Climate research has revealed that the United States has caused an estimated $10 trillion in global economic damage over the past three decades due to its carbon emissions, making it the largest historical contributor. Studies also show that ocean eddies are amplifying climate extremes in coastal seas, and North American wildfires are burning longer and earlier due to human-caused climate change. In medical discoveries, clinical trials in the UK indicate that a single blood test can now detect approximately 50 types of cancer before symptoms appear. Researchers are also presenting new findings on drug resistance in lung cancer and early-onset colorectal cancer, and rapid bloodstream infection testing is showing promise for timely, lifesaving treatments.

The Strait of Hormuz is experiencing significant escalation today, April 18, 2026, as Iran reimposed restrictions and its Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) gunboats reportedly fired on at least two commercial vessels, including an Indian-flagged tanker and a container ship. ## SECTION 10 — Global Economy & Market Signals

**Crude Oil:** Global oil prices, which saw a significant drop on Friday, April 17, 2026, following Iran's brief declaration of the Strait of Hormuz reopening, are set for renewed volatility after Iran reimposed restrictions and fired on vessels today. Brent crude had declined to $91.45 per barrel and WTI crude to $92.77 per barrel on Wednesday, April 15, 2026, reflecting a temporary easing of geopolitical risk premium. The market remains sensitive to logistics and transit issues in the Middle East. Natural gas prices reached a 17-month low due to warmer US spring temperatures and increased storage levels.

**Equities:** US stock indexes closed higher on Friday, April 17, 2026, with the S&P 500 reaching an all-time high of 7148.39 in April 2026, and rising 1.20% from the previous session. This upward trend, observed across the Dow Jones Industrial Average and Nasdaq composite earlier in the week, reflected investor optimism amid hopes for a de-escalation in the US-Iran conflict.

**Currency:** On Friday, April 17, 2026, the US Dollar Index (DXY) reversed nearly all gains accumulated since the onset of the Iran conflict. The EUR/USD exchange rate has closely mirrored shifts in European energy prices, leading to a notable depreciation of the euro against the US dollar.

**Central Banks:** The US Federal Reserve maintained the federal funds rate steady at 3.5%-3.75% in March 2026, with policymakers still anticipating one rate cut this year and another in 2027. The next Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting is scheduled for April 28-29, 2026. Central banks in China, Indonesia, Turkey, Russia, Kazakhstan, and the Philippines are reviewing their monetary policies this week, April 18-24, 2026.

**Inflation Indicators:** US inflation surged to a two-year high of 3.3% in March 2026, driven significantly by the energy shock from the Iran war, with core CPI rising 2.6% year-over-year. The International Monetary Fund (IMF) has warned that persistently high oil prices could elevate global inflation to 6% by next year. Global real GDP growth forecasts for 2026 have been reduced to 2.4%, marking the weakest growth rate since 2009, excluding the pandemic year.

## SECTION 13 — India National (Politics & Governance)

On Saturday, April 18, 2026, the Union Cabinet, chaired by Prime Minister Narendra Modi, approved a 2% increase in Dearness Allowance (DA) for central government employees and Dearness Relief (DR) for pensioners, effective from January 1, 2026. This decision, impacting approximately 5.046 million employees and 6.827 million pensioners, will cost the exchequer ₹6,791.24 crore annually. The Cabinet also approved the creation of a "Bharat Maritime Insurance Pool" with a sovereign guarantee of ₹12,980 crore to ensure continuous maritime insurance coverage for Indian shipping. Additionally, the Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana-III was extended until March 2028 (and March 2029 for hilly areas), and two new railway projects were sanctioned at a total cost of ₹24,815 crore, including the Ghaziabad-Sitapur and Rajahmundry-Visakhapatnam lines.

In legislative news, the Constitution (One Hundred and Thirty-First Amendment) Bill, 2026, seeking to implement 33% women's reservation in an expanded Lok Sabha and State Assemblies, was defeated in the Lok Sabha on Friday, April 17, 2026, failing to secure the required two-thirds majority. Opposition parties criticized the delimitation process within the bill, while Prime Minister Modi accused them of "petty politics" for its defeat. Assembly Elections 2026 are ongoing in several states, with campaigning by Prime Minister Modi and Congress leader Rahul Gandhi intensifying. Opinion polls in Tamil Nadu suggest the DMK is leading, with actor Vijay's TVK emerging as a significant contender. The Supreme Court of India's website shows several orders uploaded today, April 18, 2026, and a recent landmark judgment clarified that Scheduled Caste status is tied to religious affiliation, primarily Hinduism, Sikhism, and Buddhism. In national incidents, a man was shot dead in a suspected militant ambush in Manipur today, and the Pune airport runway was cleared for flights after an Indian Air Force aircraft incident.

## SECTION 15 — Andhra Pradesh State Updates

For Saturday, April 18, 2026, the National Democratic Alliance (NDA) alliance parties in Andhra Pradesh, led by Chief Minister N. Chandrababu Naidu, organized a protest rally in Nidadavolu. The rally condemned the obstruction of the Women's Reservation Bill in Parliament, with Chief Minister Naidu designating April 17 as a "black day" for Indian democracy due to its defeat. Concurrently, YSRCP president and former Chief Minister Y.S. Jagan Mohan Reddy alleged a rise in crimes targeting women and children across Andhra Pradesh. He claimed that this increase signifies a deterioration of law and order within the state. The Board of Intermediate Education, Andhra Pradesh, revised its improvement rules for the upcoming 2026 Supplementary Exams. The revised rules extend the improvement facility to all second-year candidates, regardless of whether they passed or failed, allowing for subject-wise improvement.

## SECTION 18 — Deep Analysis of Most Important Breaking News

### The Strait of Hormuz Standoff and US-Iran Conflict Escalation

**What Happened:** On Saturday, April 18, 2026, Iran abruptly reversed its earlier decision to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, reimposing restrictions on the critical waterway. This action was accompanied by reports from the UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) and other sources that Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) gunboats fired upon at least two commercial vessels, including an Indian-flagged oil tanker and a container ship, attempting to transit the strait. The Iranian government cited the continued US naval blockade of its ports as the reason for its actions. This incident represents a significant escalation in the ongoing US-Iran conflict, which had seen a fragile ceasefire in the wider Middle East conflict.

**When it Happened:** The reversal and firing incidents occurred on Saturday, April 18, 2026. The initial, brief reopening of the strait was announced on Friday, April 17, 2026, following a 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah.

**Where it Happened:** The incidents took place in the Strait of Hormuz, specifically about 20-25 nautical miles northeast of Oman.

**Who is Involved:** Key actors include Iran (its military, particularly the IRGC), the United States (maintaining a naval blockade), India (whose flagged vessel was reportedly fired upon), and other international commercial shipping entities. Diplomatic efforts involving Pakistan as a mediator are also noted. 

**Why it Happened (Background Context):** The broader US-Iran conflict began on February 28, 2026, with US and Israeli airstrikes on Iran. Iran responded with missile and drone strikes and initially closed the Strait of Hormuz. A temporary ceasefire between Iran and the US, and a separate one between Israel and Hezbollah, had recently been in effect. Iran's brief reopening of the Strait on Friday was met by US President Donald Trump's insistence that the American blockade on Iranian ports would remain until a comprehensive deal, including on Iran's nuclear program, was reached. Iran's subsequent actions are a direct retaliation and an attempt to exert leverage in ongoing, albeit stalled, negotiations.

**Immediate Impact:** The immediate impact is a sharp escalation of military tensions in a critical global energy chokepoint. Oil markets, which had experienced a temporary drop in prices on Friday due to the perceived de-escalation, are expected to react with renewed volatility and upward pressure on prices. Commercial shipping faces increased risks and disruptions, leading to delays and higher insurance costs. India has already summoned the Iranian envoy to lodge a strong protest over the firing incident involving its vessel.

**Policy Implications:** This escalation underscores the fragility of current diplomatic efforts and the challenge of achieving a lasting peace settlement in the Middle East. It highlights the US policy of maintaining pressure through a naval blockade while simultaneously engaging in negotiations. For Iran, it demonstrates a willingness to use its control over the Strait of Hormuz as a strategic tool. The international community faces a renewed imperative to de-escalate tensions and ensure freedom of navigation through this vital waterway, potentially through multilateral security missions.

**Likely Future Consequences (based only on established trends):** Based on established trends, continued disruption of the Strait of Hormuz could lead to sustained higher global energy prices, impacting economies worldwide. The prolonged conflict and associated instability may further reshape global trade routes and supply chains, with countries seeking alternative energy sources and maritime passages. The region could see increased military deployments and a hardening of positions by involved parties, prolonging the conflict and its humanitarian and economic fallout. The focus on resolving the underlying issues of Iran's nuclear program and regional security is likely to intensify, possibly leading to more direct and high-stakes negotiations or further confrontations.

## SECTION 19 — This Day in History (Global)

*   **1506:** Construction of the current St. Peter's Basilica in Vatican City commenced.
*   **1775:** Paul Revere and William Dawes undertook their famous "Midnight Ride" to alert American patriots about the impending arrival of British forces, a pivotal moment preceding the American Revolution.
*   **1906:** A devastating 7.9 magnitude earthquake and subsequent fires ravaged San Francisco, California, resulting in over 3,000 fatalities and widespread destruction.
*   **1942:** During World War II, the Doolittle Raid, led by General James Doolittle, marked the first air raid by US forces on mainland Japan, targeting cities such as Tokyo and Yokohama.
*   **1951:** The European Coal and Steel Community (ECSC), a foundational precursor to the European Union, was established by the Treaty of Paris, signed by France, West Germany, Italy, Belgium, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands.
*   **1980:** Zimbabwe (formerly Rhodesia) gained its formal independence from Britain, with Canaan Banana becoming its first President.
*   **1988:** The United States initiated Operation Praying Mantis against Iranian naval forces, recognized as the largest naval battle since World War II.

---

## SECTION 20 — This Day in History (India)

- **1336:** The prominent Vijayanagara Hindu Empire was founded in the Deccan region of South India.
- **1612:** The marriage of Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan and Mumtaz Mahal, the inspiration for the Taj Mahal, was solemnized.
- **1859:** Tatya Tope, a leading figure of the Indian Rebellion of 1857, was captured and subsequently executed in Shivpuri.
- **1930:** Surya Sen, known as Masterda, led the Indian Republican Army in a significant raid on the Chittagong Armoury.
- **1950:** Vinoba Bhave initiated the Bhoodan movement from the village of Pachampally in Andhra Pradesh (now Telangana).
- **1971:** Air India's first Boeing 747 jumbo jet, named 'Emperor Samrat Ashok,' made its inaugural landing in Mumbai, a key event in Indian aviation history.
- **1983:** India achieved its first rocket success with the successful orbital launch of the Rohini satellite by the SLV-3 rocket, marking its entry into the global space race.

## SECTION 21 — This Week in Review (Last 7 Days)

In the past week, global attention has been dominated by the escalating US-Iran conflict, particularly the volatile situation in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's reversal on reopening the Strait, followed by reports of IRGC gunboats firing on commercial vessels, significantly heightened tensions and raised concerns about global energy supplies and market stability. Diplomatic efforts, including potential US-Iran talks in Pakistan, remain uncertain. Separately, a French UN peacekeeper was killed in southern Lebanon, with France blaming Hezbollah.

Economically, global oil prices experienced a sharp decline mid-week following a brief announcement of the Strait's reopening, only to face renewed uncertainty. Major stock indexes like the S&P 500 reached all-time highs amidst hopes for de-escalation. Central banks, including the US Federal Reserve, are closely monitoring inflation signals, which saw US inflation surge to a two-year high in March due to energy shocks.

Technologically, significant advancements were seen in AI and robotics, with AGIBOT launching new embodied AI products and Ukraine establishing an AI center for military applications. SpaceX and Blue Origin prepared for notable rocket launches. Breakthroughs in quantum computing for predicting complex systems were reported, and in biotechnology, new vaccine developments and "superbug" research progressed.

Politically in India, the defeat of the Women's Reservation Bill in the Lok Sabha sparked widespread debate and accusations between the ruling and opposition parties. The Union Cabinet approved a 2% DA/DR hike for central government employees and launched a new maritime insurance pool. In state news, Telangana announced new polytechnic seats and advocated for a steel plant and EV battery hub, while Andhra Pradesh saw protests over the Women's Reservation Bill and revisions to exam rules.

---

## SECTION 22 — This Month in Review (From 1st of Current Month)

Throughout April 2026, the global geopolitical landscape has been profoundly shaped by the US-Iran conflict, which began in late February. The protracted standoff in the Strait of Hormuz, characterized by intermittent closures and direct confrontation with commercial shipping, has triggered significant energy market volatility and raised the specter of a prolonged global energy crisis. Diplomatic attempts at de-escalation have faced considerable challenges, leading to sustained uncertainty. Concurrently, the conflict in Lebanon has intensified, culminating in an attack that killed a UN peacekeeper and ongoing military exchanges despite a fragile ceasefire.

Economically, the global outlook for 2026 has been downgraded, with the IMF projecting a slowdown in GDP growth to 2.4% due to the Middle East conflict and persistent high energy prices. US inflation reached a two-year high in March, largely driven by the energy shock. Central banks are maintaining a cautious stance amidst these inflationary pressures.

Technological advancements have continued at a rapid pace. Major AI developments include the release of the 2026 AI Index, the launch of new embodied AI and robotic systems, and the establishment of AI centers for military applications. Space missions by SpaceX and Blue Origin have progressed, while quantum computing research shows promise in predicting complex systems. In biotechnology, new vaccine developments for COVID-19 and RSV, alongside breakthroughs in "superbug" research, highlight ongoing efforts in global health. Biosecurity concerns have led to new US regulations targeting foreign-linked biotechnology.

In India, national politics have been dominated by the contentious debate surrounding the Women's Reservation Bill, which was defeated in the Lok Sabha. The Union Cabinet announced a 2% DA/DR hike for central government employees, established a maritime insurance pool, and extended rural road projects. State-level news in Telangana focused on educational and industrial development, while Andhra Pradesh addressed law and order issues and educational reforms.

## SECTION 24 — Key Global Indicators Snapshot

**Crude oil benchmark price (Brent/WTI):** As of April 18, 2026, Brent crude is trading around $96 per barrel and WTI between $92 and $94, balancing easing supply concerns with ongoing geopolitical uncertainty. On April 17, 2026, Brent fell to $91.87 USD/Bbl (down 7.57%) and WTI to $85.57 USD/Bbl (down 9.63%) following Iran's brief declaration of the Strait of Hormuz reopening. Prices had earlier soared due to the Iran war.

**US Dollar index direction:** The US Dollar Index (DXY) was at 98.2353 on April 17, 2026, stabilizing above 98 after falling approximately 0.5% on Friday, April 17, 2026. Over the past month, the US Dollar has weakened by 1.85%.

**Major currency signals:** The US dollar broadly weakened on Friday, April 17, 2026, particularly against the Swiss franc, Australian dollar, Japanese yen, and the euro. The EUR/USD saw an extended rise, while AUD/USD was a top performer last week.

**Global equity trend:** Global equity markets showed notable resilience in 2026, with the S&P 500 reaching new all-time highs of 7126 points on April 17, 2026. The Nasdaq also achieved new record highs. Indian markets (Nifty) ended the week with a 1.26% gain.

**Inflation indicators:** US inflation, measured by the Consumer Price Index (CPI), surged to a two-year high of 3.3% in March 2026, primarily driven by the Iran war-related energy shock. Core inflation (excluding food and energy) rose by 2.6% over the past 12 months. Year-ahead inflation expectations in the US surged to 4.8% in April 2026.

---

## SECTION 25 — Strategic Insight / Underreported Development

A significant and potentially underreported development is the dual trend of accelerating AI adoption and its complex impact on global labor markets, contrasted with a simultaneous surge in tech layoffs. While major tech companies like Oracle, Amazon, and Meta are cutting tens of thousands of jobs, explicitly citing AI-driven restructuring and efficiency goals, there is also evidence suggesting that overall job displacement due to AI has been modest so far, with new roles emerging. This paradox indicates a strategic shift where AI is not merely a tool for automation but a fundamental force reshaping entire industries and workforce compositions. The policy implication is a critical need for governments and educational institutions to rapidly implement comprehensive reskilling and upskilling programs. Failure to proactively address the evolving demand for hybrid skills (combining traditional expertise with AI, data analytics, and cybersecurity) risks creating widening skill gaps and exacerbating economic inequalities, even as AI drives productivity gains. The long-term consequence could be a deeply bifurcated labor market, where highly skilled AI-literate workers thrive, while those in automatable roles face increasing precarity, irrespective of overall economic growth.
